court: European Court of Human Rights (First Section)

facts.text: The applicant, Mrs İlkay Adalı, was born in 1944 and is a Turkish Cypriot living in the “Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus” (“TRNC”).

anchor.surface_text: The applicant,  Mrs İlkay Adalı, who was born in 1944, is a Turkish Cypriot living in the “Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus” (“TRNC”). She is represented before the Court by  Bindman & Partners, a law office in London.

facts.text: The applicant’s husband, Mr Kutlu Adalı, was a Turkish Cypriot writer and journalist who criticised the policies and practices of the Turkish Government and the “TRNC” authorities.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant’s husband, Mr Kutlu Adalı, was a Turkish Cypriot writer and journalist who was known for having written and published articles strongly criticising the policies and practices of the Turkish Government and the authorities of the “TRNC”.

facts.text: Mr Kutlu Adalı was employed as Private Secretary to Mr Rauf Denktaş between 1961 and 1972.

anchor.surface_text: Apart from his writing and journalism, Mr Kutlu Adalı had also held various civil service posts in the past. Between 1961 and 1972 he was employed as the Private Secretary to Mr Rauf Denktaş, who later became the President of the "TRNC".

facts.text: In 1972, Mr Kutlu Adalı's salary was suspended because he wished to write an article disagreeing with Mr Denktaş's policies.

anchor.surface_text: Apart from his writing and journalism, Mr Kutlu Adalı had also held various civil service posts in the past. Between 1961 and 1972 he was employed as the Private Secretary to Mr Rauf Denktaş... In 1972 the applicant’s salary was suspended because he had wished to write an article about policies of Mr Denktaş with which he disagreed.

facts.text: In 1974, Mr Adalı was appointed Head of Identity Cards Section of the Department for the Registration of the Population.

anchor.surface_text: In 1974, Mr Adalı was appointed to the post of Head of Identity Cards Section of the Department for the Registration of the Population.

facts.text: Mr Kutlu Adalı was compelled to take early retirement in 1987 at age 50.

anchor.surface_text: His career as a civil servant ended in 1987, when he was compelled to take early retirement at the age of 50.

facts.text: Between January 1980 and July 1996, unknown persons harassed Mr Kutlu Adalı with threats, machine gun attacks on his house, and threatening phone calls.

anchor.surface_text: The applicant and her husband received various threats intended to deter him from continuing to express those beliefs. Between January 1980 and July 1996 unknown persons subjected the applicant’s husband to various forms of harassment.

facts.text: On 17 March 1996, Yenidüzen newspaper published an article by Kutlu Adalı about thieves breaking into St Barnabas tomb linked to Civil Defence Organisation members.

anchor.surface_text: On 17 March 1996 the "Yenidüzen" newspaper printed an article by Kutlu Adalı about an incident in which thieves had broken into a tomb in the monastery of St Barnabas...

facts.text: After the 17 March 1996 article, Mr Adalı received frequent threatening phone calls.

anchor.surface_text: ...After the publication of this article, the editor of the newspaper received a threatening phone call from the head of the Civil Defence Organisation. Mr Adalı also began to receive frequent threatening phone calls.

facts.text: On 4 July 1996, Yenidüzen published an article by Mr Adalı criticising the “Mother Country-Child Country” policy.

anchor.surface_text: On 4 July 1996 the Yenidüzen Newspaper published another article by Mr Adalı which strongly criticised the “Mother Country- Child Country” policy of the Government of Turkey and that of the "TRNC".

facts.text: On 6 July 1996 at around 11.35 p.m., Kutlu Adalı was shot and killed in front of his house in the “TRNC” by unknown persons.

anchor.surface_text: On 6 July 1996, at around 11.35 p.m., the applicant’s husband was shot and killed in front of his house in the “TRNC” by unknown persons.

facts.text: The “TRNC” authorities refused to show the applicant her husband’s body and claimed no post mortem was carried out, though later a report was provided.

anchor.surface_text: ...The “TRNC” authorities refused to show the applicant her husband’s body. She was told by the doctor in charge of the mortuary, Dr İsmail Bundak, that no post mortem had been carried out...

facts.text: Investigation began immediately after Kutlu Adalı's death on 6 July 1996; police arrived shortly after a tip-off at 11.40 p.m.

anchor.surface_text: The Government submit that on 6 July 1996 at 11.40 p.m. a tip-off was received... Following the tip-off two Land Rovers belonging to the special unit... came to the incident scene within a short time.

facts.text: Autopsy on 7 July 1996 by Dr Ismail Bundak established cause of death as gunshot wounds.

anchor.surface_text: Following the autopsy carried out by Dr Ismail Bundak on 7 July 1996, the cause of death was identified as dismemberment of the internal organs...

facts.text: Police took statements from 33 persons on 7 July 1996 and 26 more between 8 and 31 July 1996.

anchor.surface_text: On 7 July 1996 police officers drew up a list of residents... On the same day statements were taken from thirty-three persons... Between 8 and 31 July 1996 twenty-six further statements were taken...

facts.text: Ballistics report of 6 August 1996 on 14 cartridges: 9 mm parabellum from one gun, not linked to prior cases.

anchor.surface_text: A ballistic examination was also carried out on the used cartridges. Upon the examination of 14 used cartridges, the ballistics report of 6 August 1996 stated that they were 9 mm. parabellum type...

facts.text: Coroner's inquest commenced 20 October 1998, concluded 11 December 1998, finding unidentified killers.

anchor.surface_text: The hearing in Lefkosa before the Coroner commenced on 20 October 1998... was concluded on 11 December 1998 with the delivery of the verdict. The coroner found that Kutlu Adalı was shot dead...

facts.text: Application lodged with European Commission of Human Rights on 12 September 1997.

anchor.surface_text: Having regard to the above application lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights on 12 September 1997,

facts.text: Commission’s decision of 19 October 1998.

anchor.surface_text: Having regard to the Commission’s decision of 19 October 1998,

facts.text: Oral hearing on 31 January 2002.

anchor.surface_text: Having regard to the parties’ oral submissions at the hearing on 31 January 2002,

facts.text: Applicant received anonymous call three days after murder naming Hüseyin Demirci and Orhan as responsible.

anchor.surface_text: Three days after her husband was killed, the applicant’s family received a telephone call from an anonymous caller... who gave the names of two individuals... Mr Hüseyin Demirci and a man whose first name is Orhan.

facts.text: On 8 July 1996, “Kıbrıs” newspaper reported claim by Turkish Revenge Brigade of killing Adalı.

anchor.surface_text: On 8 July 1996 the pro-“TRNC” government newspaper “Kıbrıs” reported that they had received a statement from a fascist group calling itself the Turkish Revenge Brigade claiming that they had killed Kutlu Adalı.

facts.text: Applicant requested martyr status for husband on 18 July 1996, rejected 9 September 1996.

anchor.surface_text: On 18 July 1996 the applicant requested from President Denktaş that the status of martyr be awarded to Kutlu Adalı. On 9 September 1996 her request was rejected.

facts.text: “TRNC” refused to register “Kutlu Adalı Foundation”.

anchor.surface_text: The “TRNC” regime also refused to register the foundation, which is called “Kutlu Adalı Foundation”...

facts.text: On 20 June 1997, authorities prevented applicant and daughter from crossing to southern Cyprus.

anchor.surface_text: On 20 June 1997 the public authorities prevented the applicant and her daughter from attending a meeting organised by a radio station in southern Cyprus...

facts.text: Government claims Abdullah Çatlı not in TRNC on 6 July 1996; last visit 26 April-1 May 1996.

anchor.surface_text: The Government maintain that the allegation about the involvement of Abdullah Çatlı is no more than a speculation... according to which Abdullah Çatlı’s last visit to TRNC had been between 26 April 1996 and 1 May 1996.

facts.text: Court declares application admissible on 31 January 2002.

anchor.surface_text: For these reasons, the Court by a majority Decides to join to the merits the Government’s objections... Declares the application admissible.

facts.text: Turkey exercises effective control over northern Cyprus, imputing TRNC acts to Turkey.

anchor.surface_text: In the case of Cyprus v. Turkey... Turkey’s responsibility cannot be confined to the acts of its own soldiers... but must also be engaged by virtue of the acts of the local administration...

facts.text: Applicant's daughter dismissed from bank on 12 October 1998 due to disorderly conduct, per Government.

anchor.surface_text: The Government contend that the applicant’s daughter was dismissed from her post in the Erbank due to disorderly conduct on 12 October 1998.

facts.text: Professor Bakır Çağlar allegedly threatened applicant in December 1999 about her ECHR application.

anchor.surface_text: Moreover, the applicant’s representatives informed the Court on 21 January 2000 that in December 1999 the applicant had a meeting with Professor Bakır Çağlar... allegedly told the applicant that she could be assassinated...

Article 1 sets limits on the reach of the Convention, the concept of “jurisdiction” under this provision is not restricted to the national territory of the High Contracting Parties. In addition, the responsibility of the High Contracting Parties can be involved because of acts of their authorities, whether performed within or outside national boundaries, which produce effects outside their own territory.

concepts.interpretation: Jurisdiction not restricted to territory; extends via effective control over area outside territory, imputing responsibility for acts of subordinate local administration (citing Loizidou and Cyprus v. Turkey).

anchor.surface_text: the rule of exhaustion of domestic remedies referred to in Article 35 § 1 of the Convention obliges applicants to use first the remedies that are normally available and sufficient in the domestic legal system to enable them to obtain redress for the breaches alleged.

concepts.interpretation: Remedies must be sufficiently certain in practice and theory; TRNC remedies considered domestic for Turkey; objection joined to merits (citing Ilhan, Akdivar).
Six-month period from when remedies ineffective or definitively rejected; linked to effectiveness of investigation; joined to merits.

anchor.surface_text: Article 2 of the Convention that her husband’s murder was a manifestation of a continuing practice on the part of the Turkish-controlled authorities of the “TRNC”

concepts.interpretation: Alleged failure to conduct effective investigation into murder; raises complex issues not manifestly ill-founded.

anchor.surface_text: under Article 3, 6, 8, 10, 11, 13 and 14 of the Convention that, subsequent to the death of her husband, she has been subjected to the continuing practices of monitoring, harassment, intimidation and discrimination

concepts.interpretation: Alleged ongoing harassment post-murder; complex issues joined to merits.

anchor.surface_text: under Article 6 of the Convention that because of the failure to conduct a prompt, thorough, impartial and effective investigation of the circumstances of her husband’s murder

concepts.interpretation: Denial of access to courts for compensation; lack of independence; complex issues.

under Article 10 of the Convention that her husband was killed because of his public expression of views strongly critical of the policies and practices of the Turkish Government.

concepts.interpretation: Killing as extreme interference with expression; applicant indirect victim; complex.

anchor.surface_text: under Articles 3, 6, 8, 10, 11, 13 and 14 of the Convention

concepts.interpretation: Post-death harassment including prevention of meetings; complex.

anchor.surface_text: Under Article 13 of the Convention, the applicant states that the failure of the public authorities, for which Turkey is responsible, to conduct a prompt, impartial, thorough and effective investigation

concepts.interpretation: Failure of investigation indicates no effective remedy; complex.

anchor.surface_text: under Article 14 of the Convention that the treatment they have been subjected to was due to Kutlu Adalı’s political opinions.

concepts.interpretation: Discrimination on political opinions; complex issues.

anchor.surface_text: Article 34 of the Convention that the respondent Government tried to hinder the effective exercise of her right to apply to the Court.

concepts.interpretation: Alleged threats to hinder application; complex, admissible.

anchor.surface_text: manifestly ill-founded within the meaning of Article 35 § 3 of the Convention.

concepts.interpretation: Court assesses complaints not manifestly ill-founded, declares admissible.

issues.text: Whether the matters complained of in the present application fall within the jurisdiction of Turkey within the meaning of Article 1 of the Convention

anchor.surface_text: Court recalls that, although Article 1 sets limits on the reach of the Convention, the concept of “jurisdiction” under this provision is not restricted to the national territory of the High Contracting Parties. In addition, the responsibility of the High Contracting Parties can be involved because of acts of their authorities, whether performed within or outside national boundaries, which produce effects outside their own territory.

issues.text: Whether the applicant has failed to exhaust domestic remedies as required by Article 35 § 1 of the Convention

anchor.surface_text: the rule of exhaustion of domestic remedies referred to in Article 35 § 1 of the Convention obliges applicants to use first the remedies that are normally available and sufficient in the domestic legal system to enable them to obtain redress for the breaches alleged.

issues.text: Whether the application complies with the six months’ time limit as required by Article 35 of the Convention
Whether there have been violations of Articles 2, 6, 10, 13 and 14 of the Convention concerning the murder of the applicant’s husband and the investigation into it

anchor.surface_text: The Court considers, in the light of the parties’ submissions, that in this respect, the case raises complex issues of law and fact under the Convention, the determination of which should depend on an examination of the merits of the application.

issues.text: Whether there have been violations of Articles 3, 6, 8, 10, 11, 13 and 14 of the Convention concerning the treatment of the applicant following the death of her husband

anchor.surface_text: The Court considers, in the light of the parties’ submissions that this part of the application also raises complex issues of law and fact under the Convention, the determination of which should depend on an examination of the merits.

issues.text: Whether the respondent Government tried to hinder the effective exercise of the applicant’s right to apply to the Court under Article 34 of the Convention

anchor.surface_text: Article 34 of the Convention that the respondent Government tried to hinder the effective exercise of her right to apply to the Court.

issues.text: Whether the complaints are manifestly ill-founded within the meaning of Article 35 § 3 of the Convention

anchor.surface_text: manifestly ill-founded within the meaning of Article 35 § 3 of the Convention. No other ground for declaring them inadmissible has been established.

arguments.claim: The alleged acts/omissions are not those of Turkey but concern only the autonomous 'TRNC' entity

anchor.surface_text: In their observations, the Government give a detailed historical background about the separate status of the Greek and Turkish Cypriot communities. They maintain that the alleged acts/omissions put forward in the present application are not those of the Turkish republic, but may, if substantiated, concern only the “TRNC”, which is an autonomous entity.

arguments.court_reasoning: Turkey has effective control over northern Cyprus, imputing acts of local administration (citing Loizidou and Cyprus v. Turkey)

arguments.claim: Findings in prior Commission cases and Loizidou not conclusive or precedent for jurisdiction here

anchor.surface_text: The Government further maintain that the findings of the European Commission of Human Rights in the inter State applications nos. 6780/74, 6950/75 and 8007/77 against Turkey as to jurisdiction under Article 1 of the Convention should not be taken as conclusive... Moreover, the Government contend that the Loizidou case... “cannot be a precedent for the present case

arguments.court_reasoning: Applies precedent from Loizidou and Cyprus v. Turkey on effective control

arguments.claim: Jurisdiction under Article 1 does not presume responsibility; requires proof of actual involvement

anchor.surface_text: The Government assert that the finding of jurisdiction for the purposes of Article 1 of the Convention does not create a presumption of actual responsibility. The question of imputability requires an examination of the facts and proof of actual and not presumed involvement

arguments.court_reasoning: Responsibility engaged by acts of subordinate local administration under effective control

arguments.claim: 'TRNC' not subordinate; exercises own powers, military presence irrelevant

anchor.surface_text: The Government also refute the allegation that the “TRNC” was a subordinate local administration. They maintain that there is no evidence at all that the “TRNC” authorities exercised state authority upon delegation of power from Turkey or as agents of or on behalf of Turkey.

arguments.court_reasoning: Local administration survives by virtue of Turkish support (Cyprus v. Turkey)

arguments.claim: No evidence of Turkish involvement in killing

anchor.surface_text: The Government further maintain that the applicant cannot bring a shred of evidence in support of her allegations about the involvement of Turkey in her husband’s killing.

arguments.claim: Article 1 jurisdiction extends to Turkey via effective control over northern Cyprus
Applicant failed to exhaust domestic remedies available in 'TRNC' judicial system
arguments.court_reasoning: Objection joined to merits as linked to substance

arguments.claim: No criminal complaints filed regarding threats

anchor.surface_text: As regards the threats towards the applicant, the Government maintain that the applicant has not filed any criminal complaints with the authorities.

arguments.claim: Exhaustion requires use of certain, effective remedies in theory and practice
'TRNC' remedies are domestic for Turkey; effectiveness to be assessed on merits

arguments.court_reasoning: Linked to effectiveness of remedies, joined to merits

anchor.surface_text: The applicant alleges under Article 2 of the Convention that her husband’s murder was a manifestation of a continuing practice on the part of the Turkish-controlled authorities of the “TRNC”... She also complains that the authorities failed to carry out an impartial and effective investigation into the murder

arguments.court_reasoning: Raises complex issues joined to merits, not manifestly ill-founded

arguments.claim: Investigation into murder was prompt and thorough but perpetrators unidentified; no evidence of state/political motive

anchor.surface_text: The Government refute the allegations and maintain that the “TRNC” authorities commenced an investigation into the murder of Kutlu Adalı immediately after his death however the perpetrators of the crime could not be identified. They further submit that the applicant cannot bring any evidence

arguments.claim: No effective access to courts for compensation due to flawed investigation; 'TRNC' courts not independent

anchor.surface_text: The applicant submits under Article 6 of the Convention that because of the failure to conduct a prompt, thorough, impartial and effective investigation... she has been denied effective access to the courts to determine her civil right to compensation

arguments.claim: Killed due to critical expression; applicant indirect victim

anchor.surface_text: The applicant alleges under Article 10 of the Convention that her husband was killed because of his public expression of views strongly critical of the policies and practices of the Turkish Government. She submits that his killing was the most extreme interference

arguments.claim: Failure of investigation denies effective remedy

anchor.surface_text: Under Article 13 of the Convention, the applicant states that the failure of the public authorities... to conduct a prompt, impartial, thorough and effective investigation into the circumstances of her husband’s murder indicate that those responsible... have a blinkered approach

arguments.claim: Treatment due to political opinions

anchor.surface_text: The applicant also complains under Article 14 of the Convention that the treatment they have been subjected to was due to Kutlu Adalı’s political opinions.

arguments.claim: Post-death continuing harassment, monitoring, intimidation by authorities (Arts 3,6,8,10,11,13,14)

anchor.surface_text: The applicant complains of the continuing practices of monitoring, harassment, intimidation and discrimination by the “TRNC” authorities... plain-clothed policemen... threatening phone calls... water supply cut... prevented crossing... digging machines on anniversary etc.

arguments.court_reasoning: Complex issues joined to merits, not manifestly ill-founded

arguments.claim: Post-death allegations exaggerated; no police complaints filed; contradiction in seeking protection
Threat by Prof. Çağlar to hinder application; he was former agent
Prof. Çağlar resigned, not representing Government

anchor.surface_text: The Government repudiate this allegation and submit that Professor Çağlar, who was the agent of the Turkish Government in the case of Loizidou v. Turkey, had resigned from his post and therefore he was not representing the Turkish Government in any way.

arguments.claim: Complaints raise complex issues not manifestly ill-founded; admissible

holdings.text: The Court concludes, accordingly, that the matters complained of in the present application fall within the “jurisdiction” of Turkey within the meaning of Article 1 of the Convention and therefore entail the respondent State’s responsibility under the Convention.

anchor.surface_text: The Court concludes, accordingly, that the matters complained of in the present application fall within the “jurisdiction” of Turkey within the meaning of Article 1 of the Convention and therefore entail the respondent State’s responsibility under the Convention.

holdings.reasoning_summary: Applies Article 1 jurisdiction via effective control over northern Cyprus, imputing acts of subordinate local administration (citing Loizidou v. Turkey and Cyprus v. Turkey).

holdings.text: The Court considers that this limb of the Government’s objection raises issues that are closely linked with the substance of the applicant’s complaints. Consequently, this objection concerning the exhaustion of domestic remedies should be joined to the merits of the application.

anchor.surface_text: The Court considers that this limb of the Government’s objection raises issues that are closely linked with the substance of the applicant’s complaints. Consequently, this objection concerning the exhaustion of domestic remedies should be joined to the merits of the application.

holdings.reasoning_summary: TRNC remedies regarded as domestic for Turkey per Cyprus v. Turkey; effectiveness linked to merits substance.

holdings.text: As the Government’s objection about the six months’ time limit is clearly linked to the issue of the effectiveness of the domestic remedies, the Court considers that this limb of the Government’s objection should also be joined to the merits of the case.

anchor.surface_text: As the Government’s objection about the six months’ time limit is clearly linked to the issue of the effectiveness of the domestic remedies, the Court considers that this limb of the Government’s objection should also be joined to the merits of the case.

holdings.reasoning_summary: Six-month period linked to effectiveness of investigation/remedies; joins to merits (citing Aytekin, Yaşa).

holdings.text: The Court considers, in the light of the parties’ submissions, that in this respect, the case raises complex issues of law and fact under the Convention, the determination of which should depend on an examination of the merits of the application. Consequently, the Court concludes that the complaints raised in this part cannot be declared manifestly ill-founded within the meaning of Article 35 § 3 of the Convention. No other ground for declaring them inadmissible has been established.

holdings.text: For these reasons, the Court by a majority Decides to join to the merits the Government’s objections concerning exhaustion of domestic remedies and compliance with the six months’ time limit; Declares the application admissible.

anchor.surface_text: The Court recalls in the first place that a criminal investigation may constitute an effective remedy in respect of the allegations of killing by State agents (see the Aytekin v. Turkey judgment of 23 September 1998, Reports 1998-VII, No. 92, § 83). However, this remedy may become ineffective under special circumstances

precedents.cited_proposition: criminal investigation constitutes effective remedy for allegations of killing by State agents

anchor.surface_text: The Court concludes, accordingly, that the matters complained of in the present application fall within the “jurisdiction” of Turkey within the meaning of Article 1 of the Convention and therefore entail the respondent State’s responsibility under the Convention.
